exploration_log.aha_moment: The function already handles zero-length correctly (size 0 passes the <= 64 check, ExtendCrc32cInline returns the crc unchanged). The fix is just to add an explicit early-out with ABSL_PREDICT_FALSE before the <= 64 check, marking it cold.
exploration_log.hindsight: Could also check if ExtendCrc32cInline handles the empty case and whether removing the inner <= 64 check's empty case coverage matters - but the explicit early-out is sufficient.

alternatives: Mark the existing <= 64 branch with PREDICT_TRUE and add a cold sub-branch
Add an explicit PREDICT_FALSE early return for empty
key_decisions.decision: Add ABSL_PREDICT_FALSE(buf_to_add.empty()) early return before the size <= 64 check
key_decisions.reasoning: Cleanest approach: empty input is handled with explicit early return annotated as unlikely, improving code layout for the common case

solve_notes: Added an explicit `ABSL_PREDICT_FALSE(buf_to_add.empty())` early-return guard at the top of the `ExtendCrc32c` inline function in `crc32c.h`. This moves the zero-length check to a cold code path, improving branch predictor hints and code layout for the common non-empty case.
task_text:
The `crc32c::ExtendCrc32c()` in `crc/crc32c.h` handles zero-length
input correctly but incurs an unnecessary branch in the hot path. When
hardware CRC is available, the empty-input branch is never taken by the
CPU branch predictor but still impacts code layout. Move the
zero-length check to a cold path with `ABSL_PREDICT_FALSE`.

tier_difference_reasoning: The minimum set covers only the edited function. An AI agent would additionally check ExtendCrc32cInline to verify it handles empty input correctly (so the new early-return doesn't break existing correct behavior). Without this check, the agent might worry about correctness and search for it during implementation.
